simpleMeaning: Generous or forgiving, especially towards a rival or less powerful person.
detailedMeaning: Showing or suggesting a lofty and courageous spirit; showing or suggesting nobility of feeling and generosity of mind.
examplesJson: [{"text": "The magnanimous leader forgave his rivals.", "context": ""}, {"text": "She was too magnanimous to resent all the things others had said to her.", "context": ""}]

simpleMeaning: Lasting for a very short time.
detailedMeaning: Lasting for a very short time; transient; fleeting. Often used to describe things found in nature, like insects or flowers, but also applied to abstract concepts like fame or beauty.
etymology: Late 16th century: from Greek ephēmeros (from epi 'on' + hēmera 'day').
examplesJson: [{"text": "Fashions are ephemeral.", "context": "Everyday"}, {"text": "The ephemeral joys of childhood.", "context": "Literature"}]

simpleMeaning: Fluent or persuasive in speaking or writing.
detailedMeaning: Clearly expressing or indicating something; having or exercising the power of fluent, forceful, and appropriate speech.
etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French, from Latin eloquent- 'speaking out'.
examplesJson: [{"text": "An eloquent speech.", "context": ""}, {"text": "Her glance was more eloquent than words.", "context": ""}]

detailedMeaning: Characterized by or expressing goodwill or kindly feelings; desiring to help others; charitable.
etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French benivolent, from Latin bene volent- 'well wishing'.

simpleMeaning: The occurrence and development of events by chance in a happy or beneficial way.
detailedMeaning: An aptitude for making desirable discoveries by accident. Good luck in making unexpected and fortunate discoveries.
etymology: Coined by Horace Walpole in 1754, from the Persian fairy tale The Three Princes of Serendip.
examplesJson: [{"text": "A fortunate stroke of serendipity.", "context": ""}, {"text": "It was serendipity that they met at the cafe.", "context": ""}]

detailedMeaning: Existing or being everywhere, especially at the same time; omnipresent.
etymology: Mid 19th century: from modern Latin ubiquitas (from Latin ubique 'everywhere') + -ous.
examplesJson: [{"text": "His ubiquitous influence was felt by all the family.", "context": ""}, {"text": "Smartphones have become ubiquitous in daily life.", "context": ""}]

simpleMeaning: Sweet or musical; pleasant to hear.
detailedMeaning: Sweetly or smoothly flowing; sweet-sounding. Flowing with honey; sweetened with or as if with honey.
etymology: Late 15th century: from late Latin mellifluus (from mel 'honey' + fluere 'to flow') + -ous.

simpleMeaning: Able to withstand or recover quickly from difficult conditions.
detailedMeaning: Springing back; rebounding. Returning to the original form or position after being bent, compressed, or stretched.
etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin resilient- 'leaping back', from the verb resilire.
examplesJson: [{"text": "Babies are generally far more resilient than new parents realize.", "context": ""}, {"text": "The local economy is remarkably resilient.", "context": ""}]

simpleMeaning: Too great or extreme to be expressed or described in words.
detailedMeaning: Incapable of being expressed or described in words; inexpressible. Not to be spoken because of its sacredness; unutterable.
etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French, or from Latin ineffabilis, from in- 'not' + effabilis (from effari 'speak out').
examplesJson: [{"text": "The ineffable natural beauty of the Everglades.", "context": ""}, {"text": "An ineffable joy.", "context": ""}]
